channel.title: NYT > Arts
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: How Scientifically Accurate Is ‘Project Hail Mary’? Experts Weigh In
item.description: Based on hard science fiction, a genre that prioritizes scientific accuracy, the blockbuster gets a lot right but misses a few things, experts say.
item.dc:creator: Alexis Soloski

item.title: After 11 Years in Court, Heir Reclaims a Modigliani Looted by the Nazis
item.description: A judge ruled against a holding company controlled by David Nahmad, the billionaire art dealer, which had bought the work at auction in 1996.
item.dc:creator: Graham Bowley
item.media:description: “Seated Man With a Cane,” painted by Amedeo Modigliani in 1918.

item.title: ‘Hacks’ Has the Last Laugh
item.description: As the HBO comedy finished production earlier this year, a photographer captured some of the final days on set and the showrunners looked back on its award-winning run.
item.dc:creator: Chantal Anderson and Alexis Soloski

item.title: How Did You Feel About ‘The Drama’ Plot Twist? Tell Us Your Thoughts
item.description: The sort-of rom-com stars Zendaya and Robert Pattinson as an engaged couple whose relationship is upended by a revelation that has divided audiences.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson

item.title: Seats Left Empty on Smithsonian Board as Strain With White House Persists
item.description: The terms of two governing members have expired, but their replacements have yet to be named as the institution faces President Trump’s effort to play a role in the selections.
item.dc:creator: Robin Pogrebin
item.media:description: The strains between the Smithsonian and the Trump administration can be seen in the delays in filling seats on its governing board.

item.title: U2, Anne Hathaway: 9 Songs We’re Talking About This Week
item.description: U2 surprises with an Easter EP, and Anne Hathaway puts out a convincingly delivered pop song that boasts FKA twigs as a writer.
item.dc:creator: Jon Pareles
item.media:description: U2 has continued to follow a liturgical calendar with the surprise release of a new EP on Good Friday.

item.title: Chekhov Plays for an Un-Chekhov Time
item.description: The directors Michael DeFilippis, Dmitry Krymov and Aleksandr Molochnikov all infuse their current productions with a burning, modern rage.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw

item.title: Regé-Jean Page on the Album He Considers Flawless
item.description: “It’s raw, real, sensitive, strong, vulnerable, righteous, romantic, genius,” said the “Bridgerton” actor, who stars in the new film “You, Me & Tuscany.”
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr

item.title: In ‘Burnout Paradise,’ Running on Treadmills Is Only the Half of It
item.description: In “Burnout Paradise,” four performers try ambitious tasks while jogging. If they can’t beat their run time goal, the audience gets its money back.
item.dc:creator: Erik Piepenburg and Yuvraj Khanna
item.media:credit: Yuvraj Khanna for The New York Times

item.title: Who Is the Polka-Dot Band That Plays Virtuosic Rock? Angine de Poitrine.
item.description: The virtuosic rock duo from Quebec releases its second album, “Vol. II,” this week.
item.dc:creator: Jon Pareles

item.title: Megan Thee Stallion Makes Her Return to “Moulin Rouge”
item.description: The rapper was back onstage in “Moulin Rouge” two days after being taken to a hospital after becoming ill during a performance.
item.dc:creator: Elena Bergeron
item.media:description: Megan Thee Stallion was back onstage at Broadway’s Al Hirschfeld Theater on Thursday night.

item.title: Nadav Lapid’s Film ‘Yes’ Is Intended to Anger Both Israel’s Right and Left
item.description: The director Nadav Lapid uses his work to confront his discomfort with nationalism in his home country, never more so than in “Yes.”
item.dc:creator: Esther Zuckerman

item.title: On ‘Company Retreat,’ Anthony Norman Finished the Job
item.description: The unwitting hero of this Amazon prank comedy series acted heroically in the final episode.
item.dc:creator: Alexis Soloski and Jamie Lee Taete
item.category: Jury Duty Presents: Company Retreat (TV Program)
item.media:credit: Jamie Lee Taete for The New York Times

item.title: ‘Jimmy & the Demons’ Review
item.description: The impish but thoughtful James Grashow, his sculpture and his long marriage are the subjects of Cindy Meehl’s film.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson

item.title: Carol Mullins, Who Illuminated Dance for Decades, Dies at 86
item.description: She knew nothing about lighting when the director Robert Wilson asked her to work on his shows. She then spent over 40 years as a designer for Danspace Project.
item.dc:creator: Brian Seibert
item.media:credit: George Etheredge for The New York Times
item.media:description: Carol Mullins in 2024. In 1982, she got a call from the director of Danspace Project in Manhattan, offering her a position as the organization’s resident lighting designer. It was a job that Ms. Mullins would keep until her death.

item.title: Dolores Keane, Singer Known as the ‘Soul of Ireland,’ Dies at 72
item.description: She sang of homesickness and longing, and often collaborated with American musicians like John Prine and Emmylou Harris.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:description: Dolores Keane performing in 1990. She emerged in the 1970s as part of a renaissance in the traditional music of Ireland.

item.title: ‘The Drama’ and the Secrets That Can Unravel a Relationship
item.description: In the new film, a shocking revelation upends the lives of a couple played by Zendaya and Robert Pattinson, raising the question: Are some betrayals simply unforgivable?
item.dc:creator: The Styles Desk

item.title: Pedro Friedeberg, Whose Hand Chair Drew Outsize Attention, Dies at 90
item.description: Known for his hallucinatory canvases, he cemented his fame with a chair, shaped like a hand, that was both a work of art and a pop culture curio.
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams

item.title: Five International Movies to Stream Now
item.description: This month’s picks include a feminist coming-of-age tale from India, a documentary about grasshopper harvesting in Uganda, a queer fable from Brazil and more.
item.dc:creator: Devika Girish

item.title: 8 New Movies Our Critics Are Talking About This Week
item.description: Whether you’re a casual moviegoer or an avid buff, our reviewers think these films are worth knowing about.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: What to Know About ‘The Testaments,’ a Sequel to Margaret Atwood’s ‘The Handmaid’s Tale’
item.description: “The Testaments” focuses on a younger generation coming of age inside Gilead, the religious regime first imagined in Margaret Atwood’s 1985 dystopian thriller.
item.dc:creator: Martine Thompson
item.category: The Testaments: The Sequel to The Handmaid's Tale (Book)

item.title: Ben Lerner’s ‘Transcription’ Shows Us Why the Novel Will Never Die
item.description: With “Transcription,” the writer makes a case for the vitality of the form.
item.dc:creator: Parul Sehgal

item.title: Late Night Says Goodbye to Pam Bondi
item.description: President Trump is “on a bit of a firing spree,” Jimmy Fallon said on “The Tonight Show” on Thursday, adding, “Ironically, the only staffer who has immunity is RFK Jr.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)

item.title: ‘The Pitt’ Season 2 Episode 13 Recap: Should He Stay or Should He Go?
item.description: Dr. Robby finally says what his colleagues had been thinking.
item.dc:creator: Sean T. Collins
item.media:description: Noah Wyle in “The Pitt.”

item.title: Blake Lively’s Harassment Claims Against Justin Baldoni Dismissed as Case Heads to Trial
item.description: A federal judge tossed the actress’s sexual harassment claims against Justin Baldoni in a split decision that sets the stage for a contentious trial.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.media:description: In a ruling on Thursday, a federal judge allowed only a portion of Blake Lively’s lawsuit against Justin Baldoni to go forward.

item.title: Art Gallery Shows to See in April
item.description: This week in Newly Reviewed, Travis Diehl covers Pat Oleszko’s burlesque street theater, Paul Chan’s inflatables, David Armstrong’s calm curiosity and Torbjorn Rodland’s subtle awkwardness.
item.dc:creator: Travis Diehl

item.title: Ex-Turnstile Guitarist Charged With Attempted Murder of Bandmate’s Father
item.description: Brady Ebert, a founding member of the Baltimore hardcore punk band, hit the father of Brendan Yates, the group’s lead vocalist, with his car, the police said.
item.dc:creator: Remy Tumin
item.media:description: Brady Ebert performing with Turnstile in Columbus, Ohio, in 2017. He was dismissed from the band in 2022.

item.title: ‘The Blue Trail’ Review: A Drifting Journey Into Freedom
item.description: In an imaginary Brazil, where older people are forcibly removed from society, a resolute 77-year-old seeks her independence in the Amazon.
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis

item.title: Ancient Artifacts Stolen in Dutch Museum Heist Are Recovered
item.description: The golden helmet of Cotofenesti, a highly regarded artifact from Romania, and two elaborate golden bracelets were taken in January 2025.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses
item.media:credit: Sem Van Der Wal/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The recovered golden helmet of Cotofenesti and two gold bracelets on display in the Netherlands on Thursday.

item.title: Danish Shows to Watch While We’re Still at Peace
item.description: The protector of Greenland also makes very entertaining TV.
item.dc:creator: Mike Hale

item.title: Kanye West Plays First American Concert Since 2021
item.description: After years of antisemitic behavior, the rapper formerly known as Kanye West performed his first full live show in the United States since 2021.
item.dc:creator: Emmanuel Morgan
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ye, the rapper formerly known as Kanye West, performing at SoFi Stadium on Wednesday night.

item.title: The Age-Old Obsession With Living Forever
item.description: Biohackers like Bryan Johnson seem to want to live forever. What would that be like? A London exhibition offers some thoughts.
item.dc:creator: Rosa Lyster
item.media:credit: Joanna Yee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Water from a “fountain of youth” on display in “The Coming of Age,” an exhibition at the Wellcome Collection in London.

item.title: A Senegalese Artist Who Crossed Boundaries Others Didn’t Dare
item.description: A new exhibit at the Met highlights Iba Ndiaye’s myriad influences from across the globe, but ultimately his work was all his own.
item.dc:creator: Yinka Elujoba

item.title: ‘The Truth and Tragedy of Moriah Wilson’ Review: Behind a Cyclist’s Death
item.description: A crime documentary on the murder of a professional athlete leans into exploitation.
item.dc:creator: Ben Kenigsberg
item.category: The Truth and Tragedy of Moriah Wilson (Movie)

item.title: ‘Pizza Movie’ Review: Dude, Where’s My Pie?
item.description: After taking psychedelics, college roommates, Jack (Gaten Matarazzo) and Montgomery (Sean Giambrone), experience unique side effects.
item.dc:creator: Calum Marsh

item.title: ‘The Stranger’ Review: An Unexamined Life
item.description: A man of indifference becomes a killer in this adaptation of a 1942 Albert Camus novel.
item.dc:creator: Jeannette Catsoulis

item.title: ‘Living the Land’ Review: Rural China in Transition
item.description: This drama follows a boy of 10, left in the care of relatives when his parents are lured to the city by the prospect of jobs created by a tech revolution.
item.dc:creator: Beatrice Loayza

item.title: ‘A Great Awakening’ Review: An Unlikely Founding Friendship
item.description: This film tracks the friendship of Benjamin Franklin and the Rev. George Whitefield, who brought a Protestant revival to the American colonies.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Kenny

item.title: ‘The Drama’ Review: Her Secret Is His Crisis
item.description: Zendaya and Robert Pattinson play a couple upended when one confesses to coming close to committing a horrific crime.
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis
item.media:description: Robert Pattinson, left, with Zendaya, in their well-appointed Boston apartment in the movie “The Drama.”

item.title: ‘DJ Ahmet’ Review: Finding Hidden Music in the Forest
item.description: After a young shepherd in North Macedonia finds a dance party in the woods, his life changes.
item.dc:creator: Chris Azzopardi

item.title: Gainsborough’s Powdered Wig Portraits Are Suddenly Hot in New York
item.description: The Frick gathers 25 works by the painter Thomas Gainsborough, a visual compendium of the social biggies in British society.
item.dc:creator: Deborah Solomon

item.title: Everybody Wants to Sing Nate Amos’s Songs
item.description: This Is Lorelei, the solo project from the Water From Your Eyes guitarist, has become an indie-rock phenomenon in the past year.
item.dc:creator: Lindsay Zoladz
item.category: Box for Buddy, Box for Star
item.media:credit: Mark Sommerfeld for The New York Times
item.media:description: Nate Amos of This is Lorelei and Water From Your Eyes. His album due April 17 features covers of his songs performed by Jeff Tweedy of Wilco, Hayley Williams and Tim Heidecker.

item.title: Desi Lydic Wants Trump to Take a Hint About the Ballroom
item.description: “Might I suggest: If the ballroom starts experiencing ballistic assault, maybe we just cancel the ball?” she said on “The Daily Show.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)

item.title: Eugene Mirman, ‘Bob’s Burgers’ Voice Actor, Seriously Injured in Car Crash
item.description: Mr. Mirman crashed his car on Tuesday in New Hampshire. He remains hospitalized with serious but non-life-threatening injuries.
item.dc:creator: Hannah Ziegler
item.media:description: Eugene Mirman, a comedian and actor known for his role as Gene Belcher on the animated series “Bob’s Burgers,” was hospitalized on Tuesday with serious injuries after crashing his car in New Hampshire.

item.title: Bettina Köster, a Leading Voice in Berlin’s 1980s Avant-Garde, Dies at 66
item.description: She sang lead for influential all-female bands, including Malaria!, and toured in the U.S. and elsewhere with groups like the Birthday Party and New Order.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen

item.title: The Good List: 7 Things to Add Some Delight to Your Day
item.description: A fish doorbell, jumping humanity and 1,000 days of happiness.
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch

item.title: Bravo’s ‘Summer House’ West Wilson and Amanda Batula Scandal, Explained
item.description: Will the newly announced “connection” between the co-stars West Wilson and Amanda Batula become Scandoval 2.0?
item.dc:creator: Jessica Roy
item.media:description: Amanda Batula and West Wilson released a joint statement on Tuesday saying that their “connection grew out of a genuine, longstanding friendship.”

item.title: Fosse! Robbins! Making the Case for Vintage Broadway Dance
item.description: Hubbard Street Dance Chicago and the Off Broadway revue “Gotta Dance!” shine a light on repertory that is too often overlooked.
item.dc:creator: Brian Seibert

item.title: The Best Movies and TV Shows Coming to Netflix in April
item.description: New shows of note this month include a new season of “Beef” and an animated “Stranger Things” spinoff.
item.dc:creator: Noel Murray
item.media:description: Oscar Isaac and Carey Mulligan play an unhappy couple in the second season of “Beef,” premiering on April 16.

item.title: Josefina Aguilar, Who Depicted Mexican Life in Clay, Dies at 80
item.description: Her sculpted figurines were hailed as exemplars of folk art, drawing a Rockefeller’s admiration. She continued her artistry even after losing her eyesight.
item.dc:creator: Jeré Longman

item.title: Megan Thee Stallion Rushed to the Hospital During Moulin Rouge Broadway Performance
item.description: The rapper was rushed to the hospital midway through a performance of “Moulin Rouge! The Musical” on Tuesday. She is expected to return to the show on Thursday.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:credit: Theo Wargo/Getty Images for Hot Girl Productions
item.media:description: Megan Thee Stallion made her Broadway debut in “Moulin Rouge! The Musical” at the Al Hirschfeld Theater on March 24.

item.title: Adaptations of Shakespeare’s ‘Hamlet’ and ‘Titus Andronicus’ Explore Themes of Life, Death and Joy
item.description: Two Shakespeare adaptations — Teatro La Plaza’s uplifting remix and Red Bull Theater’s gore fest — place very different values on human existence.
item.dc:creator: Laura Collins-Hughes

item.title: New York City Ballet in 2026-27: A Rare Balanchine and Ratmansky’s ‘Romeo’
item.description: Two principal dancers will say farewell in the 2026-27 season, which features the revival of Balanchine’s “Pithoprakta” and Alexei Ratmansky’s “Romeo and Juliet.”
item.dc:creator: Adam Nagourney
item.media:credit: Sasha Arutyunova for The New York Times
item.media:description: Taylor Stanley will retire from the company with a farewell performance in February.

item.title: Sadie Sink, Noah Jupe and the Wonder of Young Love, in ‘Romeo and Juliet’
item.description: The sincerity of the play’s two stars shines through in Robert Icke’s new London production.
item.dc:creator: Houman Barekat

item.title: Bruce Springsteen Brings Fiery Speeches and Songs to Minneapolis
item.description: The E Street Band opened its Land of Hope and Dreams tour on Tuesday night, where the musician asked the crowd to choose “unity over division and peace over war.”
item.dc:creator: Evelyn McDonnell
item.media:credit: Galen Fletcher for The New York Times
item.media:description: Bruce Springsteen and the E Street band onstage in Minneapolis on Tuesday night, where the group opened its Land of Hope and Dreams tour.

item.title: Jane Fonda, Jennifer Tilly and More Star in Off Broadway Shows This April
item.description: Jennifer Tilly and Daphne Rubin-Vega in “The Adding Machine,” plus Jane Fonda in an eco-musical and Cecily Strong and Corey Stoll as a couple on their first date.
item.dc:creator: Laura Collins-Hughes
item.category: Off the Record: Acts of Restorative Justice (Play)
Dear Everything: A Musical Uprising for the Earth (Play)
item.media:description: Jennifer Tilly, left, and Daphne Rubin-Vega star in “The Adding Machine,” a revival of Elmer L. Rice’s 1923 experimental drama, at the Theater at St. Clement’s in Manhattan.

item.title: ‘Star Spangled to Death’: Ken Jacobs’s History of the United States
item.description: At the Museum of Modern Art through April 7, audiences can enter and exit a screening of the 6½-hour film, which Jacobs began in the 1950s.
item.dc:creator: J. Hoberman

item.title: When Stars’ Plastic Surgery Is Played for Your Entertainment
item.description: On TikTok and Instagram, content creators play detective, trying to figure out what work has been done. But they’re really just critiquing women’s looks.
item.dc:creator: Esther Zuckerman

item.title: How ‘American Psycho’ and Marina Abramovic Fed the Met Opera’s ‘Tristan’
item.description: The creators of a new “Tristan und Isolde” production explain the influences behind every element of a crucial scene.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli
item.media:description: One scene near the end of Act I of “Tristan und Isolde” at the Metropolitan Opera contains a constellation of influences for the director, Yuval Sharon, and his collaborators.

item.title: 34 Fun Things to Do in N.Y.C. in April
item.description: This month brings Barry Manilow and Martha Graham, Earth Day and Easter, as well as a pickle tour and a little night music.
item.dc:creator: Erik Piepenburg
item.category: New York Society for Ethical Culture
item.media:description: The 1970s cult classic “The Warriors,” which is screening in the BAM film series Hoyt-Schemerhorn: Stand Clear of the Closing Doors.

item.title: The Best Movies and TV Shows Streaming in April 2026
item.description: A new Jonathan Glatzer tech satire and an adaptation of Isabel Allende’s novel “The House of the Spirits” are among this month’s highlights.
item.dc:creator: Noel Murray
item.category: The House of the Spirits (TV Program)
Star Wars: Maul - Shadow Lord (TV Program)

item.title: Lucy Sante on Collage: ‘You Have to Kill One Thing to Make Another.’
item.description: The visual historian and celebrated author of “Low Life” has two shows of recent artwork made from decades of gathering materials, a trove she slices and glues.
item.dc:creator: Walker Mimms
item.category: American Academy of Arts and Letters
item.media:credit: Mark Sommerfeld for The New York Times

item.title: 10 Short Books for Spring
item.description: These single-serving satires, family dramas and romances can be read cover-to-cover in one sun-dappled afternoon.
item.dc:creator: Calum Marsh
